ideas.Return to classical sources:
The call to **return to the sources** (*ad fontes*, "to the fountains") — to recover and study the original texts of classical antiquity and of Christianity in their authentic form and original languages — was the guiding principle of Erasmus of Rotterdam's life's work and the very soul of Renaissance **Christian humanism**, of which he was the greatest exemplar. Erasmus believed that genuine learning, piety, and reform could come only from going back to the pure wellsprings of the tradition, rather than relying on the accumulated distortions, commentaries, and corruptions of later ages.

In practice this meant, above all, a program of rigorous **textual scholarship** and philology. Erasmus devoted enormous labor to editing and purifying the foundational texts of Western culture: the writings of the classical Greek and Latin authors, the works of the early **Church Fathers** (Jerome, Augustine, and others, whose reliable editions he produced), and — most consequentially — the **Bible** itself. His landmark **1516 edition of the Greek New Testament** (the first published Greek New Testament, printed alongside his own fresh Latin translation) was a revolutionary act of scholarship: by going back to the original Greek, Erasmus exposed errors and mistranslations that had crept into the standard Latin Vulgate and made the sacred text available for critical study.

Underlying this scholarly enterprise was a religious and reforming vision Erasmus called the "**philosophy of Christ**" (*philosophia Christi*) — the conviction that authentic Christianity is found in the simple, ethical teaching of the Gospels and the early Church, not in the arid disputes of the Scholastic theologians or the ceremonial accretions of the late medieval Church. By recovering the pure sources, Erasmus hoped to renew both learning and faith. This humanist principle of *ad fontes* profoundly shaped the Renaissance, biblical scholarship, and — though Erasmus himself sought reform from within — helped prepare the ground for the Reformation.
ideas.Critique of clerical corruption:
Erasmus was one of the most influential and incisive **critics of the corruption, hypocrisy, and abuses of the late medieval Church** — of ignorant and worldly clergy, superstitious popular piety, and the empty formalism that he believed had displaced authentic Christianity. Wielding his unrivaled wit, learning, and mastery of satire, Erasmus exposed the failings of the ecclesiastical establishment while remaining, unlike Luther, committed to reforming the Church **from within** rather than breaking with it.

His criticism found its most brilliant and famous expression in the satirical masterpiece *In Praise of Folly* (1511), in which the personified figure of Folly mockingly praises, among others, the **monks, theologians, and churchmen** of the age — skewering the pedantry and hair-splitting of the Scholastic theologians, the greed, ignorance, and hypocrisy of monks and friars, the worldliness and pomp of bishops and popes, and the superstitions of ordinary religious practice (the trade in relics and indulgences, the mechanical performance of rituals, the veneration of saints for selfish ends). Throughout his writings, Erasmus contrasted this corrupt, external, ceremonial religion with the simple, inward, ethical **"philosophy of Christ"** that he found in the Gospels.

Crucially, Erasmus's aim was **reform, not revolution**. He sought to cleanse and renew the Church by education, scholarship, and moral persuasion — by returning to the sources of scripture and the Fathers, and by recalling Christians to the genuine spirit of the Gospel — rather than by schism. When Luther's revolt erupted, Erasmus, though he had sharply criticized many of the same abuses (giving rise to the quip that "**Erasmus laid the egg that Luther hatched**"), ultimately declined to join the Reformation, distancing himself from Luther's radicalism and remaining within the Catholic Church. His penetrating yet moderate critique of clerical corruption made him a pivotal figure on the eve of the Reformation and a model of humanist reform.
ideas.Free will against Luther:
Erasmus's defense of **free will** against Martin Luther, set out in his 1524 treatise *On Free Will* (*De Libero Arbitrio*), marks his decisive and public break with the Protestant Reformation and constitutes one of the most famous theological debates of the sixteenth century. Long reluctant to attack Luther directly (despite having criticized many of the same church abuses), Erasmus was finally drawn into the controversy, and he chose to make his stand on the pivotal question of the human will's role in salvation.

Against Luther's stark doctrine — that after the Fall the human will is utterly **bound and enslaved**, incapable of contributing anything to its own salvation, which depends *wholly* on God's grace and predestination — Erasmus defended a more moderate position affirming a genuine, if limited, **freedom of the will**. Drawing on scripture (which, he argued, would not exhort people to choose the good and warn against evil if they had no power to respond) and on the broad consensus of the Church tradition, Erasmus maintained that human beings, aided by grace, retain some real capacity to **cooperate** with God in the work of their salvation — to freely accept or reject the grace offered them. He also displayed his characteristic **moderation and skepticism**, cautioning against dogmatic certainty on so obscure and difficult a matter, and preferring a humble, undogmatic stance to Luther's absolute assertions.

Luther responded with fierce vigor in *On the Bondage of the Will* (*De Servo Arbitrio*, 1525), which he considered one of his best works, defending in the strongest terms the total bondage of the will and the sovereignty of divine grace. The exchange laid bare the fundamental divide between humanist and Reformation sensibilities — between Erasmus's emphasis on human moral responsibility, free cooperation, and undogmatic moderation, and Luther's uncompromising insistence on grace alone. The debate crystallized Erasmus's position as a reformer who would not become a revolutionary, and it remains a landmark in the history of theology.

works.In Praise of Folly:
*In Praise of Folly* (*Moriae Encomium*, 1511) is Erasmus of Rotterdam's most famous work and one of the most celebrated satirical masterpieces of the Renaissance — a brilliant, witty, and subversive mock-encomium that skewers the follies and abuses of his age, especially those of the Church, while raising deep questions about wisdom, foolishness, and the Christian life. Erasmus composed it, reportedly in just a few days, while staying with his friend Thomas More (to whom it is dedicated — the Latin title puns on More's name).

The work takes the ingenious form of a **speech delivered by Folly herself** (*Moria*), personified as a goddess who stands up to deliver an oration in her own praise. Through this device of ironic self-praise, Erasmus achieves a many-layered satire. Folly boasts of her universal sway over human affairs, arguing wryly that a certain amount of foolishness is necessary and even beneficial to life — that self-love, illusion, and folly make existence bearable and society function. But as the speech proceeds, its satirical edge sharpens into a biting critique of the pretensions and corruptions of society: the pedantry of grammarians and philosophers, the vanity of scholars, and — most pointedly — the **abuses of the Church**: the sophistical hair-splitting of Scholastic **theologians**, the ignorance, greed, and hypocrisy of **monks and friars**, and the worldliness of bishops and popes who neglect the true teaching of Christ.

In its remarkable final movement, however, the tone shifts, and Folly praises the "**folly of the cross**" — the Christian paradox (from St. Paul) that true wisdom appears as foolishness to the world, and that genuine faith and self-forgetting love are a divine "madness" surpassing worldly reason. Combining humanist learning, comic brilliance, and serious religious purpose, *In Praise of Folly* was enormously popular and influential, and remains a classic of both satire and Christian humanism.
works.Adages:
The *Adages* (*Adagia*) is one of Erasmus of Rotterdam's most important and popular works — a vast, continually expanding collection of **proverbs, maxims, and sayings** drawn from the classical Greek and Latin authors, gathered, translated, and explained with learned commentary. First published in 1500 as a modest collection of some 800 adages and expanded over Erasmus's lifetime into a monumental compendium of over 4,000, it was a landmark of Renaissance **humanist scholarship** and a bestseller that did much to spread classical learning throughout educated Europe.

The purpose of the *Adages* was to make the wisdom, wit, and eloquence of **classical antiquity** accessible and usable to Erasmus's contemporaries. Each entry presents a proverb or pithy saying gleaned from ancient literature, traces its origins and meaning, illustrates its use with quotations from the ancient authors, and often expands into a substantial **essay**. Many familiar expressions still current in European languages were popularized or transmitted through this work — phrases such as "to leave no stone unturned," "to call a spade a spade," "the blind leading the blind," "in the land of the blind, the one-eyed man is king," and "make haste slowly" (*festina lente*).

The *Adages* was far more than a reference book. Some of its entries grew into lengthy, forceful essays in which Erasmus expressed his own moral, political, and religious views — most famously the essay on the adage "*dulce bellum inexpertis*" ("war is sweet to those who have not experienced it"), a passionate and influential denunciation of the folly and horror of **war**, and the essay "*Sileni Alcibiadis*," on the contrast between outward appearance and inner worth. Through the *Adages*, Erasmus not only revived and diffused the treasures of classical culture but also embedded his humanist ideals of wisdom, peace, and moral reform, making it one of the most influential books of its era.
works.On Free Will:
*On Free Will* (*De Libero Arbitrio Diatribe sive Collatio*, 1524) is Erasmus of Rotterdam's most important theological treatise and the work that marked his decisive public **break with Martin Luther** and the Protestant Reformation. After years of reluctance to attack Luther directly — despite pressure from both sides and his own criticisms of church abuses — Erasmus finally entered the fray, choosing to challenge Luther on the fundamental and divisive question of the freedom of the human will in relation to divine grace.

In the treatise, Erasmus defends the reality of **free will** — the view that human beings, assisted by God's grace, retain a genuine capacity to **cooperate** with that grace and to play some real part in their own salvation, freely accepting or rejecting what God offers. Against Luther's doctrine that the fallen will is wholly **enslaved** and contributes nothing to salvation, which rests entirely on grace and predestination, Erasmus argues from scripture — pointing out that the Bible's constant exhortations, commands, warnings, and promises of reward and punishment presuppose that human beings have some power to choose — and from the long consensus of the Church Fathers and tradition. Characteristically, he also adopts a tone of **moderation and skeptical humility**, treating the question as an obscure and difficult one on which certainty is hard to attain, and cautioning against Luther's dogmatic absolutism; his very title, *Diatribe* or *Collatio* ("a discussion" or "comparison"), signals this tentative, inquiring spirit.

Luther replied the following year with the powerful and uncompromising *On the Bondage of the Will*, and the exchange became one of the defining theological confrontations of the age, exposing the deep gulf between Erasmian humanism — with its emphasis on human moral responsibility, free cooperation, ethical striving, and undogmatic moderation — and the Reformation's insistence on grace alone and the bound will. *On Free Will* thus stands as a pivotal document both of Erasmus's thought and of the parting of ways between humanism and Protestantism.
